title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a small museum. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Helen, The Welcome Desk Planner
Helen volunteered at the small museum after retiring from a travel agency, thinking she would simply greet visitors. Instead, she soon found herself coordinating the front desk. [47]The first obstacle was arranging reliable access to the storeroom, because the visitor maps and audio guides were kept behind a door only paid staff could open.[/47] On busy mornings, volunteers had to wait while school groups crowded the entrance. [48]Her years arranging complicated holidays helped her build a rota that matched guides, language skills and group bookings without double-booking anyone.[/48] She also introduced a notebook for messages that used to vanish on scraps of paper. Helen says she enjoys the moment when a visitor arrives looking doubtful and leaves asking when the next exhibition opens. The work has made her less afraid of quiet days, because a single conversation about one object can be enough to make the museum feel alive. Today.
texts.B:
### Joel, The Object Handling Volunteer
[49]Joel joined after the education officer visited his college and asked whether any history students wanted to help with school workshops.[/49] He expected to talk about battles and kings, but the sessions were much more hands-on. [50]He was surprised by the strict rules about gloves, clean tables and written records before children could touch even ordinary-looking objects.[/50] At first, he worried the rules would make the workshops feel stiff. Instead, pupils became more curious when they understood that the objects were real and needed care. Joel’s favourite session involved a Roman cooking pot, which prompted more questions than any textbook he had read. He learned to answer honestly when he did not know something and to turn uncertainty into a shared investigation. He says the museum stopped feeling like a place that stores facts and became a place where questions are allowed to grow. That freedom suited his own curiosity.
texts.C:
### Amira, The Map Redesigner
Amira, a trainee architect, began helping the museum after repeatedly getting lost inside it herself. [51]While waiting for her bus outside, she had often watched visitors come out, turn the paper map around several times and then go back in looking embarrassed.[/51] She offered to redesign the route signs before the summer exhibition opened. [55]Her new signs used clear arrows and room numbers large enough for visitors to understand the route without stopping for long explanations.[/55] [52]The worst moment came when a temporary wall was moved overnight and made half of her printed maps inaccurate.[/52] Amira spent a frantic morning correcting them with stickers. She says the experience taught her that buildings communicate, even when nobody speaks. A confusing corridor can make people feel foolish, while a clear route lets them spend their attention on paintings, fossils and stories instead of on finding the exit. That lesson stayed with her.
texts.D:
### Rob, The Sensory Visit Helper
[53]Rob offered to improve quiet visiting times because his autistic nephew loved dinosaurs but found ordinary museum afternoons overwhelming.[/53] Rob wanted families like his to feel expected rather than exceptional. He began by walking through the galleries during different events, noting echoes, flashing screens and crowded corners. [54]His hardest task was organising cleaning, trolley movements and staff briefings so they did not interrupt the low-noise sessions.[/54] Some changes were tiny, such as turning down an introductory video and leaving a side room open for breaks. Rob was delighted when his nephew spent twenty minutes sketching a fossil without covering his ears. [56]He is now training other volunteers to run the sessions so they continue even when he cannot attend.[/56] He says accessibility is not a special favour, but a way of noticing barriers that many visitors quietly tolerate. The change also reassured parents who usually hurried through exhibitions. more slowly.

questions.explanation: Helen says maps and audio guides were stored behind a door only paid staff could open.
